Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd have bursts where I'd study for hours, usually when something hands-on grabbed my attention, but then I'd drift off for days. I tried to set up regular times but my extracurriculars and just life in general kept throwing things off. I learn best when I'm doing something physical or practical, so sitting down to just read or watch lectures felt like a struggle. I'd estimate maybe half my actual studying happened in random spurts rather than any planned schedule.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines more than anything else. I'd go days without touching course materials, then cram intensely when something was due. Some weeks I'd put in solid hours, other weeks barely anything. My extracurricular activities took up a lot of mental space, so coursework often got pushed to whenever I could fit it in. It wasn't ideal, but that's just how things worked out for me.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about something new posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything I was missing or if classmates had posted something interesting in the discussions. The gamified tasks actually pulled me in more than regular content because they felt less like a chore. When I was feeling anxious about falling behind, I'd also compulsively check the LMS even if I didn't do much once I was there.
Q4: There was a stretch mid-semester where logging in became almost automatic, especially when the gamified elements kicked in. I'd check my progress, see where I ranked or what badges I could earn, and that kept me coming back. It felt less like studying and more like a game I was playing on the side. But that routine faded when things got busy with my other activities and the novelty wore off a bit.
Q5: The gamified tasks were hands-down the most useful for me because they made me actually do things rather than passively consume information. I also found the discussion posts helpful when people shared practical examples or real-world applications. Videos were okay but only when they showed demonstrations rather than just lectures. Anything that let me engage actively rather than just sit and absorb worked way better for how I learn.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the longer lecture videos. They just didn't hold my attention, and I'd find myself zoning out or getting distracted. I'd skim them at best or just skip to the parts I thought might be on exams. It's not that I didn't think they were important, I just couldn't make myself engage with them meaningfully when they were so passive.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate results or when it had some element of challenge or competition. The gamified stuff worked because I got instant feedback and could track my progress. If something felt like busy work or just checking a box, I'd lose interest fast. I needed to feel like I was actually accomplishing something tangible, not just going through motions.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different. Required stuff I'd force myself through even if I hated it, but optional things I'd only do if they seemed genuinely interesting or fun. Weirdly, I sometimes engaged more with optional gamified tasks than required readings because the optional stuff felt like my choice. When something's required, there's this pressure that makes it feel like a chore even if the content is good.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and worked through them on my tablet while I was out or during breaks from my extracurriculars. I also used YouTube videos and other external resources when the course materials weren't clicking for me. Sometimes I'd take physical notes from downloaded PDFs and study those instead of going back to the LMS. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be working through problems in a notebook or discussing concepts with friends in person, and none of that showed up anywhere online. I'd also do practice exercises from external sources or work on projects hands-on without logging anything. My best learning often happened when I was physically doing something, like building or experimenting, which obviously the LMS couldn't capture at all.
Q11: All the time. I'd download lecture slides or PDFs once, maybe watch a video once, and then work from those materials offline for days. I preferred having everything saved locally so I didn't need internet or to deal with the LMS interface. It probably looked like I barely engaged with certain materials when really I'd spent hours with the downloaded versions.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially when my anxiety spiked about falling behind or when my extracurriculars demanded more time. I'd look at everything I needed to catch up on and just feel overwhelmed, which made me avoid the LMS entirely. Sometimes the course felt disconnected from anything practical, and that made it harder to care. Those were the weeks my attendance really dropped.
Q13: Competing responsibilities hit me hardest. Between extracurriculars and just managing life, the course often got pushed down the priority list. When I was fatigued, I'd log in but just scroll mindlessly without really absorbing anything. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections entirely because facing them felt too stressful. I'd tell myself I'd come back to it later and sometimes I did, sometimes I didn't.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open, watching a video or scrolling through materials, but my mind would be somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I was just too anxious or distracted to actually focus. I'd technically be 'studying' but nothing was sticking. Those sessions felt like wasted time even though they probably showed up as engagement in the system.
Q15: I postponed readings and longer assignments constantly, even knowing they mattered. Part of it was anxiety making me avoid things that felt overwhelming. Part of it was just that I'd rather do something hands-on or more immediately rewarding. I'd rationalize that I'd do it later when I was in a better headspace, but later kept getting pushed back until deadlines forced my hand.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd be relatively inactive until something was due soon, then I'd suddenly log in constantly and cram everything in. It wasn't healthy but it worked well enough to get me through. The deadline pressure actually helped cut through my anxiety sometimes because I had no choice but to act. Without deadlines, I probably would have drifted even more.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected me, though sometimes not in great ways. Positive feedback motivated me to keep going, but critical feedback sometimes made me anxious and avoidant. I'd delay checking grades because I was nervous about what I'd see. When feedback was constructive and specific, it helped me know what to focus on. Vague feedback just left me confused about what to do differently.
Q18: The structure was a mixed bag for me. Having clear deadlines helped because I needed that external pressure. But the way content was organized sometimes felt overwhelming, like too much at once. The gamified elements added structure that actually worked for me because they broke things into smaller, achievable chunks. More flexibility might have helped but also might have let me drift even more.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement way easier because they matched how I like to learn. Having interactive elements and clear progress markers kept me coming back. What made it harder was when there were walls of text or passive video lectures with no interaction. Also, when the course structure wasn't clear about priorities, I'd waste time on less important stuff while avoiding what actually mattered.
Q20: I can totally see how that happens. Sometimes you're browsing around, clicking through things, trying to figure out what's important or just procrastinating productively. High activity doesn't mean you're actually getting work done. You might be anxious and checking things repeatedly, or confused and searching for answers without finding them. Activity and productivity aren't the same thing at all.
Q21: That actually sounds kind of like me in some ways. I didn't have the highest LMS activity but I still managed a Merit grade because a lot of my real studying happened offline. Some people are just efficient or learn better through methods that don't involve the LMS much. You can understand material deeply without logging in constantly, especially if you're working from downloaded materials or external resources.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and how often you engage with different materials. They're decent at tracking completion and time spent. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, whether you're actually learning or just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, discussions with friends, or the mental effort you're putting in. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials. For me, real engagement happens when I'm actively doing something, solving problems, applying concepts, discussing ideas. It's about being present mentally, not just physically or digitally logged in.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety affected my engagement patterns, or how my mood shifted throughout the semester. It wouldn't capture the hands-on learning I did outside the system or the conversations with classmates that helped concepts click. It also wouldn't show when I was logged in but mentally checked out, or when I was deeply engaged with downloaded materials offline. The human experience of learning is way more complex than any log can capture.
